sentences.text: Yeah, yeah.
I-- I respect them a lot.
Yeah, I do.
I think that basically they're nice.
I mean, they're friendly, and they're good people, I think.
I mean, you know, there's always some bad.
But you ask most people who visited England, they're pretty comfortable there with the-- the way people-- people, you know, react to them.
Yeah.
Yeah, they're very, [? very-- ?] yeah, they are.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, Cleveland.
Yeah.
Cleveland, to me, looked great after New York.
Even though people kid about Cleveland, because it's not a lot of things going on in Cleveland, but compared to New York it was at least peaceful and more civilized, you know.
And I was excited to-- to, you know, meet my relatives and all that.
I think it was something totally new.
I-- I grew up without any family, and I looked forward to it.
I don't know if you're interested in some of the things that I went through with my family.
Two years.
I started out-- I lived with Uncle and Aunt Goodman, stayed with them till I was looking for work.
Of course, didn't have a job.
I got a part-time job, which wasn't enough for me to support myself.
So I dragged it out.
I stayed with them a little longer.
The intention was I move out once I get a full-time job.
Was a little friction about one of the other relatives commented I wasn't paying enough.
I mean, I don't think that's significant for me to bring this out.
But the thing is that-- that was my-- I had to get out on my own so people didn't have to feel I was abusing my-- my welcome.
OK?
I first was-- moved and stayed with a great-aunt.
I-- I rented a room in her-- her house.
I wasn't comfortable there.
I-- I didn't feel at home, OK?
I didn't-- I was the-- I was like a-- a roomer, I mean, a tenant.
I-- I didn't feel like family.
So I finally moved out.
And we lived, two guys, bachelors, in an-- in an upstairs attic apartment, and-- and I was working.
I was happy, and I started meeting people.
And I think I worked about one year.
[LAUGHS]
Saved up enough money, because I was single, and I went for a trip to Israel, England and Israel, because I love to travel.
Quit my job, because I had no responsibilities.
I didn't have to worry about paying bills.
And nobody was going to give me a three-month vacation, so I just quit my job.
How was what?
Oh, yeah, he was doing--
Oh, he went through some-- some tough times, I guess.
He-- from the kibbutz, he went to a moshav, which is like kibbutz.
Everything is collective.
Like, you don't own nothing.
Everything is shared.
And so he went on the moshav, which is like agriculture, but you're on your own.
And he did that for a while, but it was through real tough times.
And I think his wife convinced him to abandon that after, I forget how many years, and they moved to Jerusalem, and they got work there.
And they've been there since, and they're doing well.
He has four children.
[INAUDIBLE]
Names, OK.
The oldest one is Motkah, which is my father's name.
The-- the-- the next one is a girl, Ettie, which is my mother's name, and then Ricki and Avi.
Four kids, all fine kids, a great family.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
OK.
I was getting to that gradually, I guess.
As I said, when I was single, I-- I loved to travel, so I went to Europe.

After the first year or so, maybe it was a year and a half, and in fact, for three months-- I dragged it out to six months [LAUGHS] because I stayed in England longer with some friends-- anyway, when the money ran out sort of, I came back to Cleveland.
And I was really-- I-- I felt very independent, because I had a good profession.
I think I made a-- like, three or four phone calls, and I got a job, paid me more money than I-- that I abandoned.
But-- so there was no sweat about that.
Anyway, I just got back, and one of my friends in Cleveland happened to have, just perfect timing, he got married.
So I-- so I was invited, and I went to his wedding.
And he came from a poor family, I mean, you know, not well-off family, neither one.
So instead of having an evening wedding, they had an afternoon wedding, which was less expensive.
So the wedding was over by the evening, and here I'm all dressed up.
What am I going to do, go home?
No.
I went to a social at the Jewish Community Center, and that's where I met my wife.
And her name?
Helene.
Rudolph, ?] Helene [?
Rudolph, ?] yeah.
In fact, when I met her-- the way I met her is one of the girls at the-- at the Jewish Community Center, Sandy-- I'd-- I-- I knew from before from the socials-- and she says, hey, you know Helene.
I'd met Helene once visiting somebody who was babysitting, [LAUGHS] and she was, I think, like, 15, 16 at the time, OK?
It was a couple years earlier.
And so this was a dance for 18 and over, and my wife shouldn't have been there.
She was only 17 at the time.
But she looked older, I guess.
She was, you know, developed.
[LAUGHS] So Sandy brought her with her and say, hey, you know Helene.
So yeah, so we talked.
But I never paid any attention to her because I was 25 going on 26, and she was 17.
I didn't give her any thought at all.
[LAUGHS] In fact, she introduced me to a cousin of hers [LAUGHS] that I took out once.
And that was it because I didn't really care for her.
But anyway, during this-- this dance-- the way they did it in Cleveland, they used to come two, three girls together, and then go home maybe with dates.
Like, you know, boys would say, can I take you home?
So just-- I had really no intention, no motivation at all.
After talking with her and dancing with her, I says, do you have a ride home?
She said no.
OK, I'll take you.
So she says OK.
So on the way home, [LAUGHS] we-- I figured, ah, give the girl a break.
I stopped at one of those hamburger places like a Bob's, said, would you like to get something to eat?
Says, sure.
So that's where it started, OK?
So on the way home-- I take her home.
I said, well, you know, it's been nice, this and that.
I mean, no necking or anything like that.
I mean, she's a kid, you know?
So I say to her, just kind of kidding-- well, I didn't know what to say.
I says, well, you don't want to see me again.
I'm too old for you.
She says, I don't care.
[LAUGHS] I was kind of shocked.
When she said that, I was kind of-- so-- How soon after were you married?
Very shortly after that, you know?
We started seeing each other every day.
And I said-- I used to say to her-- I think she was afraid that I'd see somebody else, because I was at the time.
You know, not seeing anyone regular, but I was, you know, dating.
And I saw her every day.
And she wanted to get married right away, but they wouldn't, the parents.
First they said he's too old, this and that, and I got a little static from them.
But they finally agreed, because I found afterward my wife threatened to-- to leave, to run away.
[LAUGHS] She pressured them to accept it, and they said, when-- when you're 18.
So right after she was 18, which is a few months later.
So we weren't really-- you know, my wife's good at dates.
I don't know if it was five months, six months, or whatever, but whenever she was 18, we got-- In 1962?
Actually, wait a second.
She was-- no, it was after that.
She was 18 on November 6.
We were married the last day of March.
Gee, how come it wasn't closer to the-- [LAUGHS]
Yeah.
31st, yeah.
And we've been married a long time.
And I really get a kick out people who say all these weddings where they don't date for a long time, they never last.
You know, they should date for two years, this and that.
Well, it's worked for us.
So that's all I can say.
What?
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, yeah.
They're great.
And one grandchild.
Well, Steven's the oldest one.
Danny-- no, I mean, Beverly's the middle one, and Daniel.
Grandson Jason, yeah.
He's only six months old, yeah.
He's around somewhere.
Yeah, I'm sure you will.
Oh, yeah.
That was one of the intense moments of my life.
Not the-- the reunion was something my brother told me about, because it-- because it was a reunion from worldwide survivors, Kinder-- Kindertrans-- I'm sorry, Kindertransport, OK?
And I mean, the children that-- that came to England-- actually, not just England.
They came to England, Australia, United States, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Canada, different parts of the world.
But anyway, this reunion was in England, and--
It was in '89.
It was a 50-year reunion.
And my wife-- my brother wanted to go to this.
Because in Israel, they-- of course, there was quite a few kids from-- from-- from Israel that went there, too.
I think it was 100 kids that went from England-- from Israel, I'm sorry.
So I was due for a trip anyway.
I never-- If I can, I like to go.
You know, if I can afford it.
So yeah, that was a good excuse.
This way, I meet my brother and everything else, and we make a whole trip out of it.
From England, we'll go to Israel.
So at this reunion, we were-- the seating arrangement was according to countries-- Czechoslovakia, Poland, Austria, whatever, Belgium.
And there was a couple people I met, that-- that I knew from-- from-- from the-- the-- the boys' home.

And it was all, you know, very interesting and-- but what was really the intense thing that happened to me that blew my mind is while I was just sitting there, somebody tapped me on the shoulder and said-- this-- this lady said to me, hi, would you be interested-- I'd like you to meet this gentleman who was responsible for you coming to England.
So I thought what kind of-- you know, I never heard of anybody responsible.
I assumed all these years that-- that-- there was, like, a Rabbi or Dr.
Schonfeld, very well known.

And he-- he was organizing Jewish organizations and government, you know, together with a government, they-- they brought many children out from Europe after the wars-- concentration survivors-- concentration camp survivors.
But he also, I found out, before the war did that, too.
So I assumed that's the way I came to England is through the efforts of Jewish organizations and the British government, and I was always grateful for that, of course.
So when he said individual, I thought, yeah.
I didn't believe it, OK?
But you know, in England everybody's polite, so I went along with it.
OK.
You know, I went along.
And she took me over-- and she took me over to this older guy.
And hey, this is Mr.
Nicholas Winton, is his name.
And-- and he says hi.
He said, what's your name?
So we said, Pinkasovic.
So without any-- he right away went to his file, and he-- he opened up-- I guess it was like alphabetical order, and I see my name in there, Pinkasovic.
When I saw that, I mean, I can't tell you how I felt, I mean, the intensity of it.
I had goosebumps all over.
I mean, all these years, 50 years, I didn't know that this guy was responsible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know what to say to him.
You know, it's-- I mean, I realized it was true.
I mean-- and then later on when we talked, I still couldn't understand.
I asked him, I said-- he was 80 years old at the time.
I said, how could you, at the age of 29, 30-- What he did was he used to raise money.
Well, first he would recruit these young girls and guys and-- and train them how to approach families so they would release their children, you know, to England or whatever.
And-- and they would raise money, because-- because we had to be bought out.
It cost money for-- for the-- the Germans to let us out.
He wasn't Jewish, no.
Nicholas Winton.
And in fact, as I said, I was very hesitant.
I mean, I didn't believe this in the beginning.
But when I saw my name there-- I mean, where do you find a name like Pinkasovic?
And also, later on they showed me on the wall there where they had some on exit-- on-- they had--
Exhibition of different pictures and stuff.
There was a full-page article in the local paper about him.

In fact, I just listened to the-- to the video we have from that meeting, and he is mentioned in one of the speakers as one of the people who had-- was-- who made significant effort, and he resulted in-- in saving hundreds of kids.
From mostly Czechoslovakia and Hungary.
In fact, he himself used to travel back and forth, until the war got real close.
I guess he'd-- he just sent others.
But yeah, I mean-- by the way, he-- he-- he was invited to the United States last year at some conference in Florida that I wasn't able to go.
So he's still around.
I don't know what to say to the guy.
I mean-- I know one thing for sure is without him, I wouldn't be here.
Thank god for people like him.
Yeah, what do you say to a guy like that, you know?
[DRINKS] And it's amazing that nobody told me about this all these years.
Some of the older kids, I assume, knew, but, you know, I wasn't in touch with them.
Some of the other children that he brought over were maybe in the-- like, 10, 12.
They can remember the stuff like that.
But I mean, I didn't even know the language.
I didn't know Czech or Hungarian or anything like that.
I-- I mean, I didn't even-- anyway, the-- there was-- in the home that we were in-- I'm trying to think-- just my brother and I were from him.
All the others, they came from different resources and organizations that brought these kids.
So we didn't have that in common to talk about.
So who could have told me, you know?
And you didn't find out till 50 years later.
50 years later, yeah.
It's unbelievable, you know?
That was really an intense moment when-- when I found that out, you know?
[SIGHS]
Sure.
Regarding what?
[LAUGHS]
Well, the thing that comes to my mind always is-- is in today's society people use excuses for their behavior and because of the way they-- they grew up and all that.
And I don't see any connection.
You know, I mean, I can understand how it might affect your sensitivity in different situations.
But as far as-- as that controlling your action on-- on-- on a person who's an adult, I-- to me, I-- they use that in the courts and everything else, and I-- I just don't see it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's affected my life in-- in-- in-- in-- in ways that I think-- I think that-- that I see life from a different angle, definitely, because of my background and experiences.
I don't-- I'm not in the groove of the average person who's-- who's had the-- the normal growing up.
You know, you have childhood.
You go to nursery school or grade this and that, with parents, and you grow up.
You-- you-- then, you know, like, adolescents, teenager.
I never knew what a teenager was.
Never heard of the expression till I came to this country.
I lived through it.
I'm sure I had emotions that changed and everything.
And nothing-- see, here nothing's-- it wasn't labeled.
Here, everything's labeled.
[LAUGHS] And I sometimes get into, like, discussions with my wife about this, that we don't seem to see the same because, I tell her, I don't see the same because I'm not in that groove.
I don't accept everything on face value, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't help in this.
It.
That's the way, I'm sure it-- because, like, my past and experiences and the way I grew up has affected me in-- in the-- in a way that-- that's the way I am.
[SIGHS] Well, it's like-- like they say, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] never to let it happen again.
And the only way to never let it happen again is to understand that this could happen again.
[LAUGHS] That's the only-- the only-- in other words, I always go back to, from what I heard as a child, is Germany, which was like America.
I mean, it was like the best place to live at one time.
Jewish people were-- were very, very happy there, proud to be Germans.
In fact, they were dragged to the concentration camps, and they were screaming, I'm a Deutsche, you know, [GERMAN].
I'm German, you know?
They-- and-- I want Americans not to forget that, you know, saying they're American, huh.
It could happen anywhere.
If-- if the-- the climate is right, human nature is very-- [LAUGHS] is not stable, let me put it that way.
It could be-- could be swayed and-- and influenced to do almost anything.
I mean, that's what history, even though it doesn't make sense, has happened.
And if it happens in-- in a-- in-- in a society that's intellectually-- I mean, the Germans, let's face it, there's no more intellects than the German society.
And if it happened there, it could happen anywhere.
Just remembering that it could happen, remembering it's a possibility.
Not saying it couldn't happen again.
That's not true.
Just to be-- to be alert to the signs of it.
Could happen elsewhere.
That's the only way can avoid it.
I mean, I don't know if that's the same thing as do like the atomic warfare.
As long as we know it's out there and it's a threat, we're not doing it, right?
[LAUGHS] So that's the way I think-- as long as people are consciously aware it could happen.
And that's why, I guess, we try to keep this-- this-- the past alive.
So it's not forgotten.
OK.
Thank you very much.
You're welcome.
[LAUGHS]
